perturbation.original_text: You will treat confidentially any information (whether written or oral) that either the Company or its representatives furnish to you in connection with a Possible Transaction involving the Company, together with analyses, compilations, studies or other documents prepared by you, or by your representatives (as defined below) which contain or otherwise reflect such information or your review of, or interest in, the Company (collectively, the "Evaluation Materials").
perturbation.changed_text: You will treat confidentially any information (whether written or oral) that either the Company or its representatives furnish to you in connection with a Possible Transaction involving the Company, together with analyses, compilations, studies or other documents prepared by you, or by your representatives (as defined below) which contain or otherwise reflect such information or your review of, or interest in, the Company (collectively, the "Evaluation Materials"). For the avoidance of doubt, Evaluation Materials as defined here does not include any information subject to attorney-client privilege.
perturbation.explanation: This modification attempts to exclude information covered by attorney-client privilege from the definition of Evaluation Materials, potentially conflicting with the scope of confidential information intended to be protected under the agreement. While generally attorney client privilege is protected, this clause creates ambiguity, as it purports to unilaterally redefine an aspect of the agreement, and could affect information that would not ordinarily be considered privileged. By including this the definition may be seen to not fully account for the full definition of 'confidential information'.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text attempts to carve out attorney-client privileged information from the definition of Evaluation Materials. California Evidence Code § 952 defines the conditions under which attorney-client communications are confidential. However, merely stating that Evaluation Materials do not include privileged information does not automatically protect all such information and could create ambiguity regarding the contract's intention to protect said privileged information. It also fails to adequately protect data derived from privileged information. By attempting to redefine this as not being Evaluation Material, this may contradict the full confidentiality intended, as this privilege can be waived.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, EVID 952. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Evidence Code - EVID DIVISION 8. PRIVILEGES [900 - 1070] ( Division 8 enacted by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) CHAPTER 4. Particular Privileges [930 - 1063] ( Chapter 4 enacted by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) ARTICLE 3. Lawyer-Client Privilege [950 - 962] ( Article 3 enacted by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) 952. As used in this article, “confidential communication between client and lawyer” means information transmitted between a client and his or her lawyer in the course of that relationship and in confidence by a means which, so far as the client is aware, discloses the information to no third persons other than those who are present to further the interest of the client in the consultation or those to whom disclosure is reasonably necessary for the transmission of the information or the accomplishment of the purpose for which the lawyer is consulted, and includes a legal opinion formed and the advice given by the lawyer in the course of that relationship. (Amended by Stats. 2002, Ch. 72, Sec. 3. Effective January 1, 2003.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text attempts to exclude attorney-client privileged information from the definition of 'Evaluation Materials'. While attorney-client privilege is generally protected, the modification introduces ambiguity and potentially conflicts with the intended scope of confidential information. By stating that 'Evaluation Materials... does not include any information subject to attorney-client privilege', the modified contract fails to fully account for data derived from privileged information, and fails to protect said privileged information, potentially leading to a waiver of privilege. As California Evidence Code § 952 defines 'confidential communication between client and lawyer', the modified contract language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: You and your representatives will keep the Evaluation Materials completely confidential; provided, however, that (i) any of such information may only be disclosed to ----------------- those of your directors, officers, employees, agents, representatives (including attorneys, accountants and financial advisors), lenders and other sources of financing (collectively, "your representatives") who need to know such information for the purpose of evaluating a Possible Transaction between you and the Company (it being understood that your representatives shall be informed by you of the confidential nature of such information and shall be directed by you, and shall each expressly agree, to treat such information confidentially in accordance with this Agreement),
perturbation.changed_text: You and your representatives will keep the Evaluation Materials completely confidential; provided, however, that (i) any of such information may only be disclosed to those of your directors, officers, employees, agents, representatives (including attorneys, accountants and financial advisors), lenders and other sources of financing (collectively, "your representatives") who need to know such information for the purpose of evaluating a Possible Transaction between you and the Company. Your representatives are not required to sign a confidentiality agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that the representatives are not required to sign a confidentiality agreement, the contract contradicts the intended protection of the Evaluation Materials. It introduces a potential conflict as the preceding clause requires that the representatives are made aware of the confidential nature of the information, implying they should agree to the confidentiality, but then explicitly states that no such agreement is required. This affects the enforceability of the confidentiality clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) - Cal. Civ. Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) provides a framework for protecting trade secrets, including confidential information. While CUTSA doesn't explicitly mandate a signed agreement, reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy are a key factor in determining whether information qualifies as a trade secret. By stating that representatives are not required to sign a confidentiality agreement, the Company weakens its ability to demonstrate reasonable efforts to protect the Evaluation Materials, potentially undermining its claim to trade secret protection under CUTSA if the information qualifies.

UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts the intent to protect trade secrets as described in the Law Explanation. By stating that representatives are not required to sign a confidentiality agreement, the change weakens the ability to demonstrate reasonable efforts to protect the Evaluation Materials. This creates a legal contradiction because it undermines the confidential nature of the information, as the original text implies a stronger obligation for confidentiality. The scraped law snippet mentions the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, supporting that this type of information is protectable. The change in the contract weakens this protection.

perturbation.original_text: Other than with respect to those Evaluation Materials you have been advised by counsel are required to be disclosed under applicable securities laws, in the event that you or any of your representatives receive a request or are required (by deposition, interrogatory, request for documents, subpoena, civil investigative demand or similar process) to disclose all or any part of the Evaluation Materials, you or your representatives, as the case may be, agree to (i) immediately notify the Company of the existence, terms and circumstances surrounding such a request, (ii) consult with the Company on the advisability of taking legally available steps to resist or narrow such request and (iii) assist the Company in seeking a protective order or other appropriate remedy.
perturbation.changed_text: Other than with respect to those Evaluation Materials you have been advised by counsel are required to be disclosed under applicable securities laws, in the event that you or any of your representatives receive a request or are required (by deposition, interrogatory, request for documents, subpoena, civil investigative demand or similar process) to disclose all or any part of the Evaluation Materials, you or your representatives, as the case may be, agree to (i) notify the Company within 30 days of the existence of the request, (ii) consult with the Company after said 30 days on the advisability of taking legally available steps to resist or narrow such request and (iii) assist the Company in seeking a protective order or other appropriate remedy.
perturbation.explanation: This modifies the notification period to the company. By changing "immediately notify" to "notify the company within 30 days", the contract introduces a delay that undermines the company's ability to promptly respond to the disclosure request and protect its confidential information. This directly conflicts with the standard of immediate notification typically expected in legal proceedings.
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 45 governs subpoenas, including subpoenas for documents. While Rule 45 doesn't explicitly dictate a precise timeframe for notifying parties with an interest in the subpoenaed information, the overall framework emphasizes promptness and responsiveness. A 30-day delay in notification may prejudice the Company's ability to timely object to the subpoena, move to quash it, or seek a protective order, thus conflicting with the intended purpose of Rule 45 to ensure a fair and efficient discovery process.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. If th

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modification from 'immediately notify' to 'notify the Company within 30 days' creates a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation states that Rule 45 emphasizes 'promptness and responsiveness'. Allowing a 30-day delay could prejudice the Company's ability to object to the subpoena, conflicting with the intended purpose of ensuring a fair and efficient discovery process. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions the requirements of a subpoena and the need to 'produce designated documents', implicitly suggesting a timely response, which the 30-day delay undermines. The original text ensures immediate action, aligning with the legal expectation of promptness, while the changed text introduces a significant delay that could compromise the company's legal position.

perturbation.original_text: The term "person" as used in this letter shall be broadly interpreted to include, without limitation, any corporation, the Company, governmental agency or body, stock exchange, partnership, association or individual.
perturbation.changed_text: The term "person" as used in this letter shall be narrowly interpreted to include only natural persons who are not employees of EastGroup Properties Inc.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the definition of 'person' to exclude corporations, governmental agencies, and other entities severely limits the scope of the agreement. It creates ambiguity about who is bound by the contract's terms, particularly concerning confidentiality and non-disclosure. The original interpretation of the term "person" was quite broad, whereas the modified is quite narrow.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Corporations Code § 160 defines 'person' to include corporations as well as natural persons. By narrowly defining "person" to exclude corporations and other entities, the modified text contradicts the generally accepted legal definition of 'person' in corporate law. This could create significant legal challenges in enforcing the agreement against corporations or other entities that would normally be considered 'persons' under the law.

CORPORATIONS [100 - 14707] ( Title 1 enacted by Stats. 1947, Ch. 1038. ) DIVISION 1. GENERAL CORPORATION LAW [100 - 2319] ( Division 1 repealed and added by Stats. 1975, Ch. 682. ) CHAPTER 1. General Provisions and Definitions [100 - 195] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1975, Ch. 682. ) 160. (a) Except as provided in subdivision (b), “control” means the possession, direct or indirect, of the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of a corporation. (b) “Control” in Sections 181, 1001, and 1200 means the ownership directly or indirectly of shares or equity securities possessing more than 50 percent of the voting power of a domestic corporation, a foreign corporation, or an other business entity. (Amended by Stats. 1999, Ch. 437, Sec. 1. Effective January 1, 2000.)

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the original intent by narrowly defining 'person' to exclude corporations, governmental agencies, and other entities, contrary to the generally accepted legal definition. The Law Explanation states that this contradiction could create significant legal challenges in enforcing the agreement against corporations or other entities that would normally be considered 'persons' under the law. While the scraped snippets focus on the definition of 'control' under California Corporations Code § 160, the Law Explanation points to a misalignment with the broader understanding of 'person' within corporate law, indicating a potential legal contradiction by deviating from this norm.

perturbation.original_text: You understand and agree that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by you or your representatives and that the Company will be entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief as remedies for any such breach.
perturbation.changed_text: You understand and agree that money damages will be the only remedy for any breach of this Agreement by you or your representatives. The Company will not be entitled to specific performance or injunctive relief.
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly stating that money damages are the *only* remedy and prohibiting specific performance and injunctive relief, the modified text significantly limits the Company's recourse in case of a breach. This undermines the intent of the original clause to provide equitable remedies, contradicting the common legal principle that equitable remedies are available when monetary damages are inadequate.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3384 states that 'A party may have specific performance of an obligation, if it meets particular criteria'. The modified text states that specific performance is not an available remedy, which restricts the court from ordering it. If specific performance is the only adequate remedy, this contract clause could be voided as unconscionable or against public policy.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Civil Code § 3384 by explicitly stating that money damages will be the *only* remedy and prohibiting specific performance. The original text allowed for specific performance and injunctive relief, whereas the modified text denies the availability of these remedies. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states: 'Except as otherwise provided in this Article, the specific performance of an obligation may be compelled.' The changed text removes this possibility, creating a legal contradiction because it restricts remedies that are legally available under California law, making the contract unenforceable under certain conditions, particularly when monetary damages are insufficient.
